mission.title: Install and Configure Vultr Kubernetes Engine (VKE)
mission.description: Vultr Kubernetes Engine (VKE) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment and management of Kubernetes clusters on Vultr's infrastructure. It is ideal for users looking for a hassle-free Kubernetes experience with integrated Vultr features. Key trade-offs include reliance on Vultr's infrastructure and potential costs associated with cloud resources.

steps.title: Install the Vultr CLI
steps.description:
The Vultr CLI is necessary to interact with the Vultr API for creating and managing Kubernetes clusters.

```bash

curl -LO https://github.com/vultr/vultr-cli/releases/download/v1.0.0/vultr-cli_linux_amd64.tar.gz

tar -xzf vultr-cli_linux_amd64.tar.gz

sudo mv vultr-cli /usr/local/bin/

vultr-cli version

```

> Download the latest Vultr CLI
> Extract the downloaded tarball
> Move the CLI to a directory in your PATH
> Verify the installation

steps.title: Create a Kubernetes cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new Kubernetes cluster on Vultr with the specified version and node count.

```bash

vultr-cli k8s create --region 'ewr' --version '1.30' --node-count 3 --plan 'vc2-2c-4gb'

```

> Replace 'ewr' with your desired region

steps.title: Set up kubeconfig
steps.description:
This step configures your kubeconfig file to access the newly created Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

vultr-cli k8s kubeconfig --cluster-id <your-cluster-id> > ~/.kube/config

```

> Replace <your-cluster-id> with the actual cluster ID

steps.title: Verify the cluster nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your Kubernetes cluster to ensure they are ready.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see all nodes in 'Ready' status

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Configure autoscaling and monitoring for your cluster to ensure optimal performance.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/vultr/examples/cluster-autoscaler.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/monitoring/master/monitoring.yaml

```

> Install the cluster autoscaler
> Install monitoring tools

uninstall.title: Delete the Kubernetes cluster
uninstall.description:
This step will remove the Kubernetes cluster and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss.

```bash

vultr-cli k8s delete --cluster-id <your-cluster-id>

```

> Replace <your-cluster-id> with the actual cluster ID

uninstall.title: Clean up orphaned resources
uninstall.description:
This command ensures that any orphaned resources such as load balancers or persistent volumes are removed.

```bash

vultr-cli loadbalancer delete --all

```

> Delete all load balancers

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Kubernetes cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up your data and ensure that your applications are compatible with the new version.

```bash

vultr-cli k8s upgrade --cluster-id <your-cluster-id> --version '1.31'

kubectl get nodes

```

> Replace <your-cluster-id> with the actual cluster ID
> Verify nodes are in 'Ready' status after upgrade

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to the server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if kubeconfig is not set up correctly or the cluster is not running.

**Fix:**
Ensure kubeconfig is correctly set and the cluster is active using 'vultr-cli k8s list'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources available'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough resources to schedule new pods.

**Fix:**
Scale up the cluster by adding more nodes using 'vultr-cli k8s scale --cluster-id <your-cluster-id> --node-count <new-count>'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Load balancer creation failed'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if there are issues with the Vultr API or insufficient permissions.

**Fix:**
Check your API key permissions and ensure the Vultr API is operational.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in 'Pending' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates a lack of available resources or misconfigured node selectors.

**Fix:**
Check resource requests and node selectors in your pod specifications.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kubernetes version 1.30, improved load balancer integration.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary:
You should see output similar to the following when everything is working: 'NAME                 STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
node-1               Ready    <none>   10m   v1.30.0
node-2               Ready    <none>   10m   v1.30.0
node-3               Ready    <none>   10m   v1.30.0'

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the necessary tools and resources before starting the installation.
